title: Regime Resistance against Low-Carbon Transitions: Introducing Politics and Power into the Multi-Level Perspective
author: Geels

opening_move.description: Despite growing renewable energy deployment, overall carbon emissions from electricity generation have not improved, because incumbent fossil fuel regimes actively resist low-carbon transitions. Existing MLP (multi-level perspective) scholarship on transitions focuses on green niche-innovations but under-theorizes the political power and resistance of incumbent regimes.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding how incumbent actors (coal, gas, nuclear) resist regime change through instrumental, discursive, material, and institutional power is essential for developing effective transition policy — and reveals that 'green innovation' alone is insufficient without actively destabilizing fossil fuel regimes.

rivals_engaged: transition management that ignores incumbent resistance
structuration-theory-based MLP that under-theorizes collective actors
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper works within and extends the MLP framework, arguing it requires enrichment with political economy to capture collective actors, power relations, and resistance strategies. The contribution is a typology of incumbent resistance modes (instrumental, discursive, material, institutional) illustrated through UK electricity system cases.

epistemic_mode.quantitative_methods: Time-series data on UK electricity fuel mix (coal, gas, nuclear, renewables) and CO2 emissions 1990–2012 from DECC; IEA global emissions data.
epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Case-based illustration of resistance strategies drawn from UK energy politics: Blair nuclear revival (2005–7), EDF contract negotiations, Clean Coal discourse, CCS innovation promises. Analytical framework developed conceptually; cases illustrate the typology.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Political economy framework applied to MLP; framing analysis (Snow and Benford) for discursive resistance; typological classification of power forms (instrumental/discursive/material/institutional).
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper has a theoretical contribution (enriching MLP with political economy) supported by illustrative empirical cases from the UK electricity sector. The epistemic mode is normative-with-qualitative-empirical where the UK electricity cases illustrate the analytical typology rather than test a hypothesis.

case_type.case_name: UK electricity system — coal, gas, nuclear regime resistance (1990s–2013)

normative_stake.domain: science and technology studies, energy policy, transition studies

confidence_notes: This paper is primarily an analytical/policy paper within transition studies (STS, energy policy) — unusual for TCS but in the Energy & Society special issue. The epistemic mode is normative-with-qualitative-empirical: the paper develops a conceptual typology of resistance modes supported by UK electricity sector illustrations. The normative stake is medium: the conclusion has direct policy implications (destabilize fossil regimes, not just support green niches). The paper performs an immanent critique of the MLP tradition from within.
